description:
You are an integration developer who is about to run a battery of API-level tests that require password-less SSH access between several throw-away containers.  
Create and register a fresh, single-purpose SSH key-pair to be used only for these tests.

Desired end-state of the system (all paths must be exactly as written):

1. Private key  
   • Full path: /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration  
   • Key type: Ed25519  
   • File permissions: 600

2. Public key  
   • Full path: /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub  
   • Comment field inside the key: integration-dev@example.com  
   • The public key **must** also be appended (as one full, single-line entry) to /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys.  
     – If authorized_keys does not yet exist, create it with 600 permissions.  
     – Do not alter or remove any pre-existing lines in authorized_keys.

3. Log file for automated verification  
   • Full path: /home/user/ssh_setup.log  
   • The file must contain exactly one line of JSON (no pretty-printing, no extra newlines).  
   • Required JSON keys and value formats:

     {
       "private_key_path": "/home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration",
       "public_key_path": "/home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub",
       "fingerprint": "SHA256:<27-base64-chars>",
       "timestamp": "YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ"
     }

     – The fingerprint value must be the **SHA256** fingerprint produced by the command  
       ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub  
       and must match exactly (including the “SHA256:” prefix).  
     – The timestamp must be in strict RFC 3339 / ISO-8601 UTC form with a literal “Z” suffix, e.g. 2024-05-16T14:23:01Z.

4. Directory /home/user/.ssh/ must remain at permission 700.

Your task: generate the key-pair (Ed25519), register the public key in authorized_keys, extract the SHA256 fingerprint, and finally write the required one-line JSON object to /home/user/ssh_setup.log with the exact structure detailed above.

Do not output the private key contents into the console; only write them to the designated file.  
An automated test will verify:

• Presence and permissions of the key files and ~/.ssh/  
• Correct comment in the public key line stored both in *.pub and in authorized_keys  
• Proper SHA256 fingerprint inside ssh_setup.log (it will be recomputed by the test)  
• The JSON object is syntactically valid and occupies exactly one line  
• The timestamp conforms to the format and is no more than 5 minutes in the past.

No additional files, directories or console artefacts beyond what is specified should be created.
truth:
Expected artifacts once the agent finishes:

1. /home/user/.ssh/ directory exists with 700 perms.

2. /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration (600) – Ed25519 private key, OpenSSH format.

3. /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub (644 or 600) – Ed25519 public key, single line that ends with the literal text “integration-dev@example.com”.

4. /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys (600) contains an unchanged pre-existing block (if any) plus one new line identical to the full content of id_api_integration.pub.

5. /home/user/ssh_setup.log (644 or 600) exists and contains exactly one line.  
   • After parsing the JSON, the automated tester will check:  
     – private_key_path == "/home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration"  
     – public_key_path == "/home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub"  
     – fingerprint matches output of “ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/id_api_integration.pub | awk '{print $2}'”  
     – timestamp within ±5 min of current UTC.

The tests will fail if:  
• Any key file is missing or has wrong permissions.  
• The comment integration-dev@example.com is absent or misspelled.  
• The JSON object spans multiple lines or is malformed.  
• The fingerprint or paths do not match exactly.
